interface_chemistry.polar_discontinuity: The BaO layer is nominally neutral; the LaO layer carries +1 charge. This creates a polar interface similar to LaAlO3/SrTiO3. Charge transfer of ~0.5 e per interface unit cell is expected to avoid the polar catastrophe. This charge transfer could dope the CuO2 or NiO2 planes, which is potentially beneficial for superconductivity.
interface_chemistry.interdiffusion_risk: Moderate. Ba-La intermixing at interface is thermodynamically possible.
interface_chemistry.oxidation_state_concerns: Ni formal valence in LaNiO2 is +1 (unusual, only achievable by topotactic reduction). At the interface, Ni may oxidize to +2 if O migrates from the cuprate block.

formal_charges.CuO2_trilayer: Cu2+ (optimally doped with excess O in Hg layer)

interface_chemistry.polar_discontinuity: Same BaO-LaO polar interface as Candidate 1. However, La3Ni2O7 already has mixed Ni valence (Ni2+/Ni3+), so the charge transfer at the interface may be partially accommodated by the existing mixed-valence state of the nickelate block.
interface_chemistry.interdiffusion_risk: Higher than Candidate 1 due to Ca in the cuprate block. Ca-La mixing at the interface is possible.
interface_chemistry.oxidation_state_concerns: Ni in La3Ni2O7 is nominally +2.5 (mixed Ni2+/Ni3+). Hg1223 requires careful oxygen stoichiometry for optimal doping. Interface may disrupt the delicate charge balance of both blocks.

interface_chemistry.polar_discontinuity: Same BaO-LaO polar interface as Candidates 1 and 2. Simpler than Candidate 2 (no Ca). Charge transfer at the polar interface may dope the CuO2 plane.
interface_chemistry.interdiffusion_risk: Lower than Candidate 2. No Ca to intermix. Simpler interface chemistry.
interface_chemistry.oxidation_state_concerns: La3Ni2O7 has mixed Ni2+/Ni3+ valence. Interface may modify the Ni valence state, potentially enhancing or suppressing superconductivity in the nickelate block.

mbe_feasibility.cuprate_growth: Hg-based cuprates are challenging for MBE due to Hg volatility (vapor pressure ~0.1 Torr at 300 C). Pulsed laser deposition (PLD) is more common. Molecular beam epitaxy of Hg-cuprates has been demonstrated but requires sealed Hg atmosphere or post-anneal Hg incorporation.
mbe_feasibility.nickelate_growth: LaNiO2 is obtained by topotactic reduction of LaNiO3 films grown by MBE or PLD. LaNiO3 MBE growth is well-established (Schilling et al., Bauer et al.). Topotactic reduction with CaH2 or Al capping is routine.
mbe_feasibility.interface_sharpness: Moderate. Sequential growth possible but Hg incorporation is the bottleneck.
mbe_feasibility.overall: Challenging but not impossible. LaNiO2 side is well-controlled; Hg-cuprate side needs specialized setup.

mbe_feasibility.cuprate_growth: Same Hg volatility challenges as Candidate 1, plus the multilayer Hg1223 structure (3 CuO2 planes + 2 CaO spacers) is more complex. Each CuO2 plane must nucleate correctly in sequence.
mbe_feasibility.nickelate_growth: La3Ni2O7 RP bilayer films have been grown by MBE and PLD (Sun et al. 2023, Puphal et al. 2023). Growth control is improving rapidly.
mbe_feasibility.interface_sharpness: Lowest of all candidates. Many layers to control precisely.
mbe_feasibility.overall: Most challenging. Both blocks are complex multilayer structures. Proof-of-concept only.

mbe_feasibility.nickelate_growth: Same as Candidate 2 (La3Ni2O7 films are achievable).
mbe_feasibility.interface_sharpness: Best of all candidates. Simpler cuprate block (single CuO2 plane) reduces the number of layers to control.
mbe_feasibility.overall: Most MBE-realistic. La3Ni2O7 growth is established; Hg1201 is the simplest Hg-cuprate. Recommended first experimental target.
